nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; inhabits subtidal zones from 0–60 m depth (typically 3–18 m). Found anchored to gorgonians, black coral trees, mangroves, seagrass meadows, rocky reefs, kelp, and eelgrass beds. Reported as nocturnal.

diet.notes: Primarily frozen or live mysid shrimp; also has been documented accepting enriched brine shrimp (Artemia), amphipods, and small crustaceans from live rock. Captive-bred individuals tend to accept frozen mysis more readily than wild-caught specimens. Generally advised to feed two or more small portions per day. Slow feeders that can be outcompeted by faster-moving tankmates.

summary: The largest seahorse of the Eastern Pacific, Ingen's Seahorse (H. ingens) is generally considered an advanced species best kept in a dedicated species tank with ample hitching posts, gentle flow, and multiple daily feedings of frozen mysis shrimp.
careNotes:
Hippocampus ingens (Pacific / Giant / Ingen's Seahorse) is commonly regarded as an advanced-care species requiring a dedicated aquarium — fast-moving or aggressive tankmates have been known to out-compete seahorses for food and cause chronic stress. A tall tank with minimum ~35 gallons (133 L) for a pair is generally advised; adding approximately 20 gallons per additional pair is recommended. Gentle, low-to-moderate flow is essential; strong circulation typical of reef setups tends to stress this species. Ample hitching posts — gorgonians, artificial branches, macroalgae — are important for natural resting behaviour.

Temperature guidance varies between sources: FishBase wild-environment records span 19–28.5°C; aquarium husbandry sources (LiveAquaria, general seahorse guides) suggest keeping the upper limit to ~23–25°C to reduce Vibrio bacterial infection risk. A practical recommended range of 20–26°C is used here; many experienced keepers suggest keeping closer to the lower end of that range (around 21–23°C). pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, specific gravity 1.021–1.025 are widely cited.

Adult size also varies by source: FishBase and Animal Diversity Web record up to 30 cm TL; MarineBio notes up to 36 cm has been reported; most captive individuals reach 12–19 cm. The range {min: 12, max: 30} is used here reflecting the most commonly cited figures.

Tank minimum volume: LiveAquaria states 30 gal (114 L) for a pair; broader seahorse husbandry guides recommend 40 gal (151 L) for large species; averaged to approximately 35 gal (133 L) here.

LiveAquaria lists this species as 'Venomous: Yes'. This appears to be a standard tag applied to the seahorse category page and is not corroborated by FishBase, MarineBio, Wikipedia, or Animal Diversity Web, none of which describe venom. The species does have rigid bony plates and spines; this is noted for awareness but the 'venomous' designation is treated as uncertain.

IUCN status: Vulnerable (VU), CITES Appendix II. Captive-bred specimens strongly preferred; wild-caught individuals have difficulty accepting prepared foods and show poor long-term survival.

Variants and trade names: sold as 'Ingen's Seahorse', 'Pacific Seahorse', 'Giant Seahorse', and 'Gigantes Seahorse'. Coloration highly variable — green, reddish-maroon, gray, yellow, gold, and brown individuals all reported.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; males carry eggs in a ventral brood pouch. Gestation has been reported at 14–15 days, with brood sizes of up to 2,000 reported (though 60–400 is more typical). Sexual maturity tends to occur around 3–8 months of age. Courtship involves elaborate displays lasting several days. Captive breeding has been documented and is achievable, though fry require live zooplankton (rotifers, copepods) and frequent feedings. Generally classified as a difficult species to breed in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Ingen's Seahorse, Captive-Bred. Provided temperature (68–77°F / 20–25°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), adult size (10 in / 25.4 cm), minimum tank (30 gal), care level (Difficult), diet, and compatibility details. The collection-page URL in the manifest was navigated to the individual product page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Syngnathidae / Syngnathiformes), maximum adult size (30 cm TL), temperature range (19.1–28.5°C), native range (Eastern Pacific: California to Peru, Galápagos), IUCN status (Vulnerable), CITES Appendix II listing, and gestation period (14–15 days).

additional.name: MarineBio
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (typical 13–19 cm; max reported 36 cm), lifespan (3–5 years), breeding (brood size 60–400, maturity at 8–10 months), diet (mysids, small crustaceans), and native range. Temperature not specified. Size max discrepancy: MarineBio 36 cm vs FishBase/Wikipedia 30 cm; 30 cm used as max (more commonly cited).

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13–30 cm), lifespan (3–5 years), breeding (brood sizes up to 2,000, maturity 3–6 months), diet (Artemia and mysids documented). Confirmed IUCN Vulnerable status and 50% population decline 2007–2012.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Pacific seahorse)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range (Eastern Pacific from Baja California to Chile), adult size (12–19 cm typical, max 30 cm), habitat (subtidal to 60 m, nocturnal), CITES Appendix II. Supplementary source for range and taxonomy verification.

fish.breedingNotes: Male broods eggs in a ventral pouch; gestation 14–15 days; brood size typically 60–400 (up to 2,000 reported). Fry require live zooplankton. Captive breeding is achievable but demanding.
